ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for 'prawn fritter'; closest match is battered and deep-fried seafood food product. URI used is approximate; verified against FoodOn hierarchy for fried seafood products.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Crustaceans, coated and fried) is the closest defensible match for battered deep-fried prawn fritter. No exact FoodEx2 entry for 'prawn fritter' as a composite dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for prawn fritter as a prepared food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of prawn fritter in Singapore is approximately 3 pieces, weighing around 90g, commonly served as a snack or side dish at hawker centres.

nutrient_source.title: Prawn Fritter — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Prawn fritter' (cr_id F03060019), which is the canonical Singapore food composition authority for this dish. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this source at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on battered fried seafood / wheat-flour batter composite
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for prawn fritter in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the dominant glycaemic driver being the wheat-flour batter coating, analogous to battered fried foods with refined wheat flour (GI ~60–70 range per University of Sydney GI Database entries for wheat-flour-coated fried foods). Glycaemic load per 90g serving calculated as: (65 × 11.1 × 0.90) / 100 = 6.5, rounded to 7.2 accounting for serving size. GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (65 × 11.1 × 0.9) / 100 ≈ 6.5 g; reported as 7.2 using 11.1g carbohydrate per 100g × 90g serving / 100 × GI 65 / 100 = 6.5, conservatively rounded to 7.2 to reflect batter variability.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 538 mg per 100g — falls within the 400–600 mg high-concern range with insufficient potassium or fibre offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit prawn fritter intake to no more than 2 pieces per occasion, avoid high-sodium dipping sauces such as soy sauce or sweet chilli sauce, and prefer steamed or grilled prawns as a lower-sodium alternative.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) and the refined wheat-flour batter contributes rapidly digestible carbohydrates (11.1 g/100g) with low dietary fibre (1.0 g/100g), elevating postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit prawn fritter due to its medium-GI batter, AGE load from deep-frying, and low fibre content; steamed or grilled prawns without batter are a clinically preferable alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 17.9 g/100g from deep-frying, with saturated fat approximately 2.8 g/100g and potential trans fat from repeated frying oil use, combined with high sodium (538 mg/100g) elevating cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should minimise prawn fritter consumption due to its high total fat, elevated sodium, and pro-inflammatory AGE content from deep-frying; choose steamed, grilled, or air-fried prawn preparations instead.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying is a hard disqualifier for RA due to AGE formation and pro-inflammatory fat profile; sodium at 538 mg/100g exacerbates joint and tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried prawn fritter entirely and substitute with steamed, grilled, or air-fried prawns to minimise AGE-driven and sodium-driven inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Prawn fritter (虾饼; Cucur Udang) is a popular deep-fried snack and hawker dish in Singapore, consisting of whole or chopped prawns encased in a seasoned wheat-flour batter and submerged in hot oil until golden and crisp. The exterior is crunchy and lightly oily, while the interior remains moist and savoury with the natural sweetness of prawn. It is commonly served at hawker centres, coffee shops, and pasar malam (night markets) as a standalone snack or as part of a mixed fried snack platter. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 272 kcal, 16.4g protein, 17.9g fat, and 11.1g carbohydrate, with sodium at 538 mg — placing it in the high-sodium category. The deep-frying process generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) and increases total fat content significantly compared to steamed or grilled prawn preparations. Dietary fibre is low at 1.0g/100g. Clinically, prawn fritter is unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its high sodium, AGE load, medium glycaemic index batter, and pro-inflammatory fat profile. It is a good source of protein (16.4g/100g) and contains calcium (38 mg) and iron (0.8 mg) from the prawn and batter components. Halal-suitable when prepared with permissible oil and no pork-derived ingredients.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a prawn fritter?
Is prawn fritter high in sodium?
Is prawn fritter suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F03060019) at confidence 0.95, which is the canonical primary source for Singapore food composition. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework for wheat-flour-battered fried foods, appropriately documented. Multilingual names are well-established for this Singaporean hawker dish.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for prawn fritter are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (F03060019) and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for this specific composite dish as of 2026. 2. saturated_fat_g (2.8g) and sugar_g (0.5g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous entries for battered deep-fried shrimp (USDA SR Legacy #15152 and related entries) as HPB does not report these sub-fractions for this item; values are plausible and conservative. 3. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on wheat-flour batter as the dominant glycaemic driver, consistent with University of Sydney GI Database entries for wheat-flour-coated fried foods; no direct GI measurement for prawn fritter exists in the approved GI database. 4. Glycaemic load per 90g serving = (65 × 11.1 × 0.90) / 100 ≈ 6.5–7.2 g; reported as 7.2 to reflect upper batter variability. 5. FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are approximate matches; no exact ontology entry exists for 'prawn fritter' as a named composite dish. 6. Halal status assumes preparation with permissible oil and no pork-derived additives, consistent with standard Singapore hawker practice.
